content.text: An alert that wakes someone at 3 a.m. should also tell them exactly what to do next.
Alert-to-runbook linking

is the standard that guarantees every actionable alert carries a direct link to a current, tested runbook, so the responder spends their energy fixing the problem rather than hunting for instructions.
What it is
Alert-to-runbook linking is a policy and tooling standard requiring that each page-severity alert include a
runbook URL

in its annotations, pointing to a document that describes the alert's meaning, the likely causes, the diagnostic steps, and the remediation actions. The standard covers three things: a required schema (which annotation fields every alert must carry), a runbook template (a consistent structure so responders know where to look), and a validation gate (automation that blocks alerts with missing or dead runbook links).

For agentic AI on-call, runbooks encode agent-specific recovery actions — failing over to a backup model provider, flushing a poisoned retrieval cache, disabling a misbehaving tool, or rolling back a prompt template — that a generalist responder would not otherwise know.
Why it matters

Under incident pressure, cognitive load is the enemy. A responder who must first discover what an alert means, then search a wiki for stale instructions, loses the critical early minutes of an incident. Incident-response practice consistently shows that a linked, current runbook reduces mean-time-to-repair, especially for infrequent alerts where the responder has no memory of the last occurrence. Linking also democratises on-call: a newer engineer can execute a well-written runbook for an alert they have never seen, widening the pool of people who can hold the pager.
Benefits
Lower mean-time-to-repair because remediation steps are one click from the page.
Reduced dependence on tribal knowledge and a small set of veteran responders.
Consistent response quality regardless of who is on-call.
Runbook links double as living documentation that is exercised on every incident.
Validation gates prevent alerts from shipping without a runbook, keeping coverage complete.
Trade-offs and limitations
Runbooks rot: a link to an out-of-date runbook can be worse than none, sending responders down the wrong path.
Writing and maintaining runbooks is real ongoing work that competes with feature delivery.
Over-scripted runbooks can encourage rote execution without understanding, missing novel failure modes.
A rigid validation gate can block a genuinely urgent alert from shipping over a missing link.
Runbooks for rare alerts are the most valuable and the least frequently exercised, so most likely to be stale.
How it works

The standard defines a required annotation schema on every page-severity alert: a human summary, the runbook URL, the owning team, and the SLO the alert defends. A linter runs in CI over the alert-rule definitions and fails the build if a page-severity rule lacks a runbook annotation or if the URL does not resolve. Each runbook follows a fixed template so responders always find the same sections in the same order: symptoms, impact, diagnosis, remediation, escalation, and rollback. A periodic link-checker crawls all runbook URLs and files a ticket for any that return an error or have not been reviewed within a set interval.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Pipeline linking alert rules to validated runbooks with a CI gate

content.text: Alert rules carry a runbook annotation validated by a CI linter and a scheduled link-checker. When the alert fires, the paging tool renders the URL directly in the notification. The responder opens a runbook that follows the standard template. Post-incident feedback updates the runbook, closing the loop so the document improves each time it is used.
When to use it
Every page-severity alert in a production on-call rotation should have a linked runbook.
Especially valuable for rare, high-impact alerts where responder memory is weakest.
When onboarding new on-call engineers who lack historical context.
Ticket-severity alerts may carry a lighter linked note rather than a full runbook, since they are handled without time pressure.
How to implement
Define the required annotation schema and publish a runbook template with fixed sections.
Add a CI linter that fails the build when a page-severity alert lacks a runbook_url annotation.
Backfill runbooks for existing page-severity alerts, prioritising the most frequently fired.
Deploy a scheduled link-checker that verifies every runbook URL resolves and was reviewed recently.
Render the runbook URL prominently in the paging tool's notification payload.
Add a post-incident step that updates the runbook with anything the responder learned.
Track runbook coverage (percent of page alerts with a valid link) as a program metric.
Examples

# Alert rule with required runbook annotation
- alert: AgentToolCallErrorsHigh
  expr: |
    sum(rate(agent_tool_calls_total{status="error"}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(agent_tool_calls_total[5m])) > 0.1
  for: 10m
  labels: {severity: page, team: agent-platform}
  annotations:
    summary: "Tool-call error rate above 10% for 10m"
    slo: "agent-tool-success-99"
    runbook_url: "https://runbooks.internal/agent/tool-call-errors"
Real-world example

An agent-platform team enforced runbook linking after a postmortem found that a rare vector-store failover alert had no documentation, and the one engineer who understood it was on holiday; the incident ran 90 minutes longer than necessary. They adopted a template, wrote a linter into CI, and backfilled 34 page-severity runbooks over a sprint. The linter blocked three subsequent alert additions that lacked links until authors supplied them.

Six months later a similar vector-store failover recurred. The on-call engineer — a different person seeing the alert for the first time — followed the linked runbook's remediation steps (drain traffic, promote replica, rebuild index) and resolved it in 12 minutes. The link-checker had also flagged two runbooks as unreviewed for 180 days, prompting a refresh before they were needed.
Common mistakes
Linking to a wiki homepage or a broad dashboard instead of a specific, actionable runbook.
Writing the runbook once and never revisiting it, so it drifts from the current architecture.
Skipping the CI gate, so new alerts ship without runbooks and coverage silently erodes.
Runbooks that describe symptoms but omit concrete remediation commands the responder can run.
No ownership field, so a stale runbook has no clear owner responsible for fixing it.
Best practices
Enforce the runbook link with a CI gate; make missing links a build failure, not a suggestion.
Use a fixed runbook template so responders always find the same sections in the same order.
Include copy-pasteable commands and expected outputs, not just prose descriptions.
Run a scheduled link-checker and review-date checker; treat stale runbooks as defects.
Update the runbook as the last step of every incident it was used in.
Performance and scalability

The linting and link-checking overhead is negligible: the CI linter runs in seconds over a few hundred rules, and the scheduled checker performs one HTTP request per runbook. The real scaling concern is human: as the alert count grows, so does the runbook maintenance burden. Combat this by keeping the page-alert set small (a benefit of symptom-based alerting) and by co-locating runbooks with the code that owns the alert, so they are updated in the same change. Coverage as a percentage is the metric to watch; it should stay at or near 100% for page-severity alerts.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Prometheus annotations
Carry runbook_url
Standard annotation field
CI linter (custom)
Enforce link presence and resolvability
Fails build on violation

Runbook hosting
Version-controlled, reviewable docs
PagerDuty / Opsgenie
Render link in page

Files tickets automatically
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Responder speed

Security and reliability

Runbooks can contain sensitive operational detail — internal hostnames, break-glass procedures, failover credential paths — so host them behind authentication and audit access. A runbook that instructs destructive actions (index rebuild, cache flush) should state preconditions and a rollback clearly, because a responder under pressure will execute it literally. Ensure the runbook store's availability is independent of the systems it documents: a runbook hosted only inside the failing cluster is useless during that cluster's outage. Mirror critical runbooks to an out-of-band location the responder can always reach.
Mental model

Think of an alert-plus-runbook like an aircraft warning light wired to a checklist. Pilots do not improvise when a warning illuminates; they reach for the printed checklist tied to that exact indicator and execute it step by step, because human memory degrades under stress. The warning light without the checklist is just anxiety; the checklist without the light is never consulted. The value is in the guaranteed pairing — and in the discipline of keeping the checklist current and tested.
The runbook template in practice

A consistent template is what makes a linked runbook usable under stress: the responder learns one shape and applies it to every alert. The standard sections are symptoms (what the alert means and how to confirm impact), diagnosis (the ordered checks that narrow the cause), remediation (the concrete commands to resolve it), escalation (who to pull in and when), and rollback (how to undo a risky action). Each remediation step should be copy-pasteable and paired with the expected output so the responder can tell whether it worked.

The template also carries metadata used by automation: an owner, a last-reviewed date, and the alert names it serves. The link-checker reads these to file a refresh ticket when the review date ages out, so the template is not just documentation but the data model the validation gate operates on.

# Runbook: Agent tool-call error rate high

**Owner:** agent-platform   **Last reviewed:** 2026-06-01
**Alerts:** AgentToolCallErrorsHigh
**SLO defended:** agent-tool-success-99

## Symptoms
Tool-call error rate > 10% for 10m. Users see failed actions
or the agent apologising and retrying. Confirm on the
"Tool success rate" dashboard panel.

## Diagnosis
1. Check which tool dominates errors:
   `sum by (tool) (rate(agent_tool_calls_total{status="error"}[5m]))`
2. Is it one downstream dependency or all tools?
3. Check the dependency's own status page / health endpoint.

## Remediation
- Single tool failing: disable it via feature flag
  `flagctl disable tool.<name>`  (agent falls back gracefully)
- All tools failing: check the egress proxy / network path.

## Escalation
Page the owning service if a single downstream is down > 15m.

## Rollback
Re-enable the tool flag once the dependency recovers:
`flagctl enable tool.<name>` and watch the panel return to green.

A runbook in this shape turns an unfamiliar 3 a.m. page into a guided procedure. The responder confirms the symptom, runs the diagnosis queries verbatim, applies the flag, and knows exactly how to roll back — none of which depends on having seen this alert before.
Keep the section order identical across every runbook so responders navigate by muscle memory.
Pair each remediation command with its expected output so success is unambiguous.
Carry owner and last-reviewed metadata that the link-checker uses to file refresh tickets.
Prefer reversible remediations (feature flags) and always document the rollback.
List the exact diagnosis queries, not a description of what to look at.
State escalation triggers as concrete time-and-condition thresholds.
Closing the loop after an incident

attrs.alt: Feedback loop where each incident improves the runbook used to resolve it

content.text: The runbook improves fastest when updating it is the final, mandatory step of any incident it was used in. The responder — who just experienced exactly where the runbook helped and where it fell short — captures those gaps while they are fresh, and the review resets the last-reviewed date. Over a few incidents the runbook converges on a genuinely reliable procedure.
Key takeaways
Every page-severity alert must carry a direct link to a specific, current runbook.
Enforce the link with a CI gate so coverage cannot silently erode.
Use a fixed runbook template with symptoms, diagnosis, remediation, escalation, and rollback.
Include copy-pasteable commands, not just prose, so responders act without translation.
Run a scheduled link- and review-date checker; treat stale or dead runbooks as defects.
Host runbooks with availability independent of the systems they document.
